articles.title: Millions of dementia cases could be avoided, but personalized education is the key to actual change
articles.summary: While massive public awareness campaigns reach millions, they rarely inspire real lifestyle changes. A new review reveals that personalized, community-driven educational programs are far more effective at helping people adopt habits that reduce their dementia risk.
articles.aiSummary: While massive public awareness campaigns reach millions, they rarely inspire real lifestyle changes. A new review reveals that personalized, community-driven educational programs are far more effective at helping people adopt habits that reduce their dementia risk.
articles.aiSummaryEn: While massive public awareness campaigns reach millions, they rarely inspire real lifestyle changes. A new review reveals that personalized, community-driven educational programs are far more effective at helping people adopt habits that reduce their dementia risk.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some brains resist Alzheimer's
articles.summary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.

articles.title: Neuroscientists shed light on the illusion of learning from short videos
articles.summary: A new study reveals that consuming fragmented, fast-paced media on social platforms hinders the brain’s ability to form long-term memories. While the format grabs sensory attention, it disrupts the neural networks required to absorb facts effectively.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that consuming fragmented, fast-paced media on social platforms hinders the brain’s ability to form long-term memories. While the format grabs sensory attention, it disrupts the neural networks required to absorb facts effectively.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals that consuming fragmented, fast-paced media on social platforms hinders the brain’s ability to form long-term memories. While the format grabs sensory attention, it disrupts the neural networks required to absorb facts effectively.

articles.title: People hide psychopathy but highlight narcissism when trying to look good
articles.summary: A new study reveals that when asked to fake a good impression, people hide psychopathic and sadistic traits but actually increase their narcissistic responses. The findings show how different toxic personalities navigate social pressure and deception.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that when asked to fake a good impression, people hide psychopathic and sadistic traits but actually increase their narcissistic responses. The findings show how different toxic personalities navigate social pressure and deception.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals that when asked to fake a good impression, people hide psychopathic and sadistic traits but actually increase their narcissistic responses. The findings show how different toxic personalities navigate social pressure and deception.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: People with migraines experience restless legs syndrome at twice the normal rate
articles.summary: About 20 percent of migraine patients also experience restless legs syndrome, a rate double that of the general population. A new meta-analysis highlights the specific clinical markers and shared brain pathways linking these two disabling neurological conditions.
articles.aiSummary: About 20 percent of migraine patients also experience restless legs syndrome, a rate double that of the general population. A new meta-analysis highlights the specific clinical markers and shared brain pathways linking these two disabling neurological conditions.
articles.aiSummaryEn: About 20 percent of migraine patients also experience restless legs syndrome, a rate double that of the general population. A new meta-analysis highlights the specific clinical markers and shared brain pathways linking these two disabling neurological conditions.

articles.title: Neurotic individuals suffer worse medical side effects, and psychologists just pinpointed why
articles.summary: A new psychology study reveals why some people experience more medical side effects. Individuals who naturally lean toward negative emotions tend to anticipate worse treatment outcomes, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy known as the nocebo effect.
articles.aiSummary: A new psychology study reveals why some people experience more medical side effects. Individuals who naturally lean toward negative emotions tend to anticipate worse treatment outcomes, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy known as the nocebo effect.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new psychology study reveals why some people experience more medical side effects. Individuals who naturally lean toward negative emotions tend to anticipate worse treatment outcomes, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy known as the nocebo effect.

articles.title: Your brain can start dreaming while you are still awake, and scientists just mapped how it happens
articles.summary: A new study in Cell Reports reveals that the boundary between wakefulness and sleep is highly porous. Researchers found that humans can experience bizarre, dream-like states while awake and engage in rational planning while asleep.
articles.aiSummary: A new study in Cell Reports reveals that the boundary between wakefulness and sleep is highly porous. Researchers found that humans can experience bizarre, dream-like states while awake and engage in rational planning while asleep.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study in Cell Reports reveals that the boundary between wakefulness and sleep is highly porous. Researchers found that humans can experience bizarre, dream-like states while awake and engage in rational planning while asleep.

articles.title: Behavioral Scientist’s Summer Book List 2026
articles.summary: For your intellectual travels this summer, we’ve curated a baker’s dozen of behavioral science books newly published this year. Explore the mysteries of life and consciousness, glimpse the power of constraints and the games that define our lives, navigate life’s cliffs, and find the places that will make you who you are. — Heather Graci, Editor and Evan Nesterak, Editor-in-Chief Exploring life, death, and consciousness “Life begins, with a spark of sentience catalyzed by chemistry and chance, and then it ends, with death,” writes Maria Popova. “The great mystery is not the fable of what lies b

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's kills brain cells
articles.summary: Researchers have identified a previously overlooked mechanism of brain cell death that appears to play a major role in Alzheimer's disease and frontotemporal dementia. The finding could lead to new treatments aimed at slowing neuron loss by interrupting the process before cells are destroyed.
articles.aiSummary: Researchers have identified a previously overlooked mechanism of brain cell death that appears to play a major role in Alzheimer's disease and frontotemporal dementia. articles.title: The link between an athletic physique and upward social mobility for male refugees
articles.summary: A new study suggests that an athletic physique is associated with a higher likelihood of male Afghan refugees dating women with German citizenship, highlighting how physical traits might function as social currency for newcomers facing legal instability.
articles.aiSummary: A new study suggests that an athletic physique is associated with a higher likelihood of male Afghan refugees dating women with German citizenship, highlighting how physical traits might function as social currency for newcomers facing legal instability.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study suggests that an athletic physique is associated with a higher likelihood of male Afghan refugees dating women with German citizenship, highlighting how physical traits might function as social currency for newcomers facing legal instability.

articles.title: Higher education is less associated with economic parity for women in highly hierarchical cultures
articles.summary: Higher education is often viewed as a great equalizer. However, new research suggests the association between a college degree and women's economic equality is significantly weaker in countries that favor rigid social hierarchies and traditional gender roles.
articles.aiSummary: Higher education is often viewed as a great equalizer. However, new research suggests the association between a college degree and women's economic equality is significantly weaker in countries that favor rigid social hierarchies and traditional gender roles.

articles.title: Texting a stranger works better to fight loneliness than talking to an AI chatbot
articles.summary: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.
articles.aiSummary: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Can an artificial intelligence bot cure feelings of isolation? A new psychology experiment finds that while AI companions can offer emotional support, only daily texting with a real human peer actually reduces loneliness over time.

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Air Traffic Controller
articles.summary: Sequencing departures like a moving puzzle, scrambling when a jet takes off without clearance, and catching what radar misses with a glance out the window with Michael Rejent, an air traffic controller. articles.summary: Hugging a mother through an anxiety attack, bidding for the best routes, and absorbing strangers' confessions at 35,000 feet with Linda Beall, a flight attendant. articles.title: More than 50 percent of adults worry about their libido, new study finds
articles.summary: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.
articles.aiSummary: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Everyday stressors, mental health challenges, and relationship dynamics play a major role in how people perceive their sexual desire. New research reveals that these factors lead more than half of adults to worry about their libido.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.aiSummary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

articles.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
articles.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

articles.title: When do humans reach their psychological peak? A new study points to late midlife
articles.summary: While raw processing speed declines after your 20s, a combination of emotional intelligence, accumulated knowledge, and mature personality traits pushes optimal human functioning into late midlife. New research reveals the ideal age for complex decision-making.
articles.aiSummary: While raw processing speed declines after your 20s, a combination of emotional intelligence, accumulated knowledge, and mature personality traits pushes optimal human functioning into late midlife. New research reveals the ideal age for complex decision-making.

articles.title: Democrats are disproportionately driving the rise in angry congressional tweets, study finds
articles.summary: A recent study of over two million tweets suggests members of Congress are increasingly using angry rhetoric on social media. Researchers found that Democratic lawmakers are disproportionately driving this trend of digital outrage to build their political brands.
articles.aiSummary: A recent study of over two million tweets suggests members of Congress are increasingly using angry rhetoric on social media. Researchers found that Democratic lawmakers are disproportionately driving this trend of digital outrage to build their political brands.
